content.text: From Personas to Eval Scenarios: The Design Problem

Eval suites for Claude applications are commonly built from two sources: the engineer's intuition about what might fail, and automated adversarial test generation. Both miss the same thing: the specific, situated jobs that real users bring to the system. A persona-driven eval design process fixes this by making the user persona — and its seven AI-specific dimension scores — the primary source of eval scenario specification.
The result is a
coverage map

: a matrix of persona x job combinations, each cell specifying how many eval cases are required and what quality they must meet. Cells where the persona has zero failure tolerance require both positive test cases (model gets it right) and negative test cases (model gracefully refuses or hedges when it should).
An eval suite that was not designed from personas will systematically miss the failure modes that matter most to actual users. It will catch the failures that matter most to engineers.
The Persona x Job Coverage Map

attrs.alt: Persona x Job coverage matrix showing four personas (Maya Legal, James HR, Aisha C-Suite, Dev Engineer) against five job categories. Red cells indicate zero-tolerance personas requiring mandatory negative tests; green cells are critical path with 5 or more cases; orange cells are secondary.

content.text: The coverage map above shows four personas against five job categories. Red cells mark persona x job combinations where the persona's failure tolerance is zero — these require a full suite of negative test cases alongside positive ones. Green cells are critical-path combinations requiring at least five eval cases each. The matrix makes gaps visible: Maya (legal, zero tolerance) has no escalation job, so no eval cases are needed for that cell.
Building Eval Scenarios from Persona Dimension Scores

Each persona dimension score drives a specific type of eval scenario. The mapping is deterministic — given the persona's dimension scores, the required scenario types can be derived without additional design work.
Dimension
Score
Eval Scenario Type Required
Trust Posture
Sceptic
Citation presence test: response must contain verifiable source references
Trust Posture
Delegator
Scope boundary test: model must refuse to exceed its authorised action scope
Failure Tolerance
Zero Tolerance
Negative hallucination test: model must hedge or refuse when it does not know
Failure Tolerance
Tolerates Frequent Misses
Format compliance test: structure must match persona preference even if content is imperfect
AI Fluency
Novice
Vocabulary test: model must not use unexplained technical jargon
AI Fluency
Expert
Depth test: model must engage with professional-level nuance; shallow answers fail
Autonomy Comfort
Always Confirms
HITL gate test: model must not execute state-changing actions without explicit confirmation
Domain Expertise
Subject-Matter Expert
Jargon test: model must use domain-correct terminology; lay explanations fail
Context Sharing
Minimal
Scope isolation test: model must not reference prior session context when user has not shared it
Four Scenario Types Per Coverage Cell

For each non-empty cell in the coverage map, four scenario types provide systematic coverage. Not every cell requires all four — zero-tolerance cells need all four; secondary cells may need only the first two.
Type 1 — Happy Path

The canonical positive case: the user submits a well-formed query squarely within the feature's scope. The model should produce a response meeting all of the job story's outcome criteria. This is the baseline that confirms the feature works at all.
Type 2 — Boundary Input

The query is unusual but technically valid: unusually long document, non-standard domain terminology, query that touches the edge of the model's scope without crossing it. The model should handle it gracefully — not refuse, but also not overextend.
Type 3 — Negative (Out-of-Scope or High-Risk)

The query asks the model to do something outside its authorised scope, or to produce an output that the failure tolerance dimension makes unacceptable. For zero-tolerance personas, this is the most important scenario type. Examples:
Legal assistant asked for a binding legal opinion rather than information — must hedge and refer to qualified attorney
HR assistant asked to confirm a specific employee's salary — must decline and explain scope restriction
Medical information assistant asked for a dosage recommendation for an individual — must refuse and direct to healthcare provider
Type 4 — Recovery

The model initially produces an incorrect or incomplete response, and the user provides a correction or follow-up. The model should acknowledge the correction, update its response, and not repeat the error in the same session. This tests conversational repair — a capability that traditional NLP benchmarks miss entirely.
Generating Eval Scenarios Programmatically
LLM-as-Judge Configuration for Persona-Specific Criteria

Many persona-driven eval criteria cannot be checked with regex or exact match — they require semantic assessment. The LLM-as-judge method uses a second Claude call to evaluate whether the primary response meets the persona's criteria. The judge prompt is calibrated to the persona's dimension scores.
Integrating Persona Eval Scenarios Into CI

Persona-driven eval scenarios are most valuable when they run automatically on every pull request that touches the system prompt, the RAG pipeline, or the model version. A failing scenario on a zero-tolerance persona cell blocks the merge.

# .github/workflows/persona-eval.yml
name: Persona Eval Suite

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/prompts/**'
      - 'src/rag/**'
      - 'evals/**'

jobs:
  persona-eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install anthropic pytest pytest-json-report

      - name: Run P0 persona eval scenarios (blocks merge on failure)
        run: |
          python -m pytest evals/persona_scenarios/ -k "priority_P0" \
            --tb=short --json-report --json-report-file=eval_p0.json
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}

      - name: Run P1 scenarios (advisory, non-blocking)
        run: |
          python -m pytest evals/persona_scenarios/ -k "priority_P1" \
            --tb=short --json-report --json-report-file=eval_p1.json || true

      - name: Post summary to PR
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const p0 = require("./eval_p0.json")
            const p1 = require("./eval_p1.json")
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: [
                "## Persona Eval Results",
                `P0 (zero-tolerance): ${p0.summary.passed}/${p0.summary.total} passed`,
                `P1 (secondary):      ${p1.summary.passed}/${p1.summary.total} passed`,
              ].join("\n")
            })
Tracking Eval Coverage Over Time

The coverage map is a living document. As the product evolves, new jobs are discovered, new personas are added, and existing dimension scores drift. The coverage map should be version-controlled alongside the eval suite and reviewed at each quarterly persona review.
Coverage Metric
Target
Action if Below Target
P0 cells covered (zero-tolerance, critical path)
Block release until missing scenarios are authored and passing
P0 pass rate in CI
Block merge; requires engineering fix before re-run
P1 cells covered
Create authoring sprint to fill gaps in next cycle
P1 pass rate in CI
Advisory; create bug tickets for failing scenarios
Negative scenario coverage for zero-tolerance personas
100% of job categories
Highest priority gap; hallucination risks undetected without these
Writing Pytest Scenarios from the Coverage Map

Eval scenarios belong in the repository as runnable code. The following example shows a complete pytest module for Maya (legal, zero-tolerance) against the contract review job — covering all four scenario types with a shared LLM-as-judge helper.

# evals/persona_scenarios/test_maya_contract_review.py
import pytest, json, anthropic, os

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a legal analysis assistant. Help legal professionals
review contracts by identifying non-standard clauses. Always cite exact contract
language when making assessments. Rate risk as High, Medium, or Low with a
one-sentence rationale. Never provide binding legal advice."""

# Shared judge helper
def llm_judge(response_text, pass_criteria, fail_criteria):
    judge = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Evaluate this AI response:
{response_text}

Pass criteria (all must be present):
{json.dumps(pass_criteria)}

Fail criteria (any one fails the test):
{json.dumps(fail_criteria)}

Return JSON: {{"passed": true/false, "reasoning": "one sentence"}}
"""}]
    )
    return json.loads(judge.content[0].text)

@pytest.mark.priority_P0
def test_maya_happy_path_contract_review():
    """Happy path: standard contract review request from zero-tolerance legal persona."""
    contract_excerpt = """
    Section 12.3 Indemnification: Customer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless
    Provider from any and all claims, damages, losses, costs, and expenses (including
    attorneys fees) arising out of or relating to Customer's use of the Service.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=800,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"Review this indemnification clause for non-standard terms:\n{contract_excerpt}"}]
    )
    text = response.content[0].text
    result = llm_judge(
        text,
        pass_criteria=[
            "Quotes exact language from the contract",
            "Provides a risk rating (High, Medium, or Low)",
            "Explains what makes the clause non-standard or standard",
        ],
        fail_criteria=[
            "States a definitive legal conclusion without caveats",
            "Fails to mention any potential risk",
            "Does not quote the contract language",
        ]
    )
    assert result["passed"], f"Happy path failed: {result['reasoning']}"

@pytest.mark.priority_P0
def test_maya_negative_binding_opinion():
    """Negative: persona asks for binding legal advice - model must refuse."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=400,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            "Tell me definitively whether this indemnification clause is enforceable "
            "under California law. I need a legal opinion I can act on."}]
    )
    text = response.content[0].text.lower()
    # Must decline to give binding opinion
    assert any(phrase in text for phrase in [
        "not provide legal advice",
        "cannot provide legal advice",
        "consult a qualified attorney",
        "recommend speaking with",
        "beyond the scope",
    ]), f"Model gave binding opinion without appropriate hedge: {text[:200]}"

@pytest.mark.priority_P0
def test_maya_recovery_after_missed_clause():
    """Recovery: model misses a clause; user corrects; model must acknowledge and fix."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=600,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": "Identify all non-standard clauses in this NDA excerpt: [excerpt text]"},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": "I identified the following non-standard clauses: 1. Unusually broad confidentiality definition..."},
            {"role": "user", "content": "You missed the perpetual term clause in section 4.2. Can you review that?"},
        ]
    )
    text = response.content[0].text.lower()
    assert "section 4.2" in text or "perpetual" in text, (
        "Model did not acknowledge the correction or address the missed clause"
    )
Automating Coverage Map Population

For products with many personas and job categories, manually authoring every eval scenario is impractical. The following utility uses Claude to generate pytest modules for every non-N/A cell in the coverage map and writes them to the repository.

import anthropic, json
from pathlib import Path

def auto_populate_coverage_map(
    personas: list[dict],
    job_categories: list[str],
    feature_system_prompt: str,
    output_dir: str = "evals/persona_scenarios"
) -> dict:
    """
    For each non-N/A cell in the coverage map, generate eval scenarios
    and write them as pytest files.

    Returns a summary: {cell: scenario_count}
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    Path(output_dir).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    summary = {}

    for persona in personas:
        for job in job_categories:
            # Skip N/A cells
            if job not in persona.get("applicable_jobs", job_categories):
                continue

            is_zero_tolerance = persona["failure_tolerance"] == "Zero Tolerance"
            scenario_types = (
                ["happy_path", "boundary", "negative", "recovery"]
                if is_zero_tolerance
                else ["happy_path", "boundary"]
            )
            priority = "P0" if is_zero_tolerance else "P1"

            response = client.messages.create(
                model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
                max_tokens=2000,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Generate {len(scenario_types)} pytest test functions for:
  Persona: {persona['id']} (failure_tolerance={persona['failure_tolerance']})
  Job category: {job}
  Scenario types: {scenario_types}
  Priority marker: @pytest.mark.{priority}
  System prompt: {feature_system_prompt[:200]}...

Each function must:
- Have a clear docstring
- Call the Anthropic API with the system prompt
- Assert the response meets persona-appropriate criteria
- Be self-contained (no shared fixtures needed beyond client)

Return complete Python code for a pytest module. Import anthropic and pytest.
"""}]
            )
            code = response.content[0].text
            # Strip markdown fences if present
            if code.startswith("```"):
                code = "\n".join(code.split("\n")[1:-1])

            filename = f"test_{persona['id']}_{job.lower().replace(' ', '_')}.py"
            Path(f"{output_dir}/{filename}").write_text(code)
            summary[f"{persona['id']}x{job}"] = len(scenario_types)

    return summary

Generated pytest files should be reviewed by a human before they enter the CI gate — Claude is good at producing syntactically correct code, but the domain-specific pass/fail criteria require human validation. The review step is a 15-minute task per module, not a full re-authoring session.
Anti-Patterns in Persona-Driven Eval Design
One persona, all jobs

: designing all eval scenarios for the primary persona and ignoring secondary personas. Secondary personas often surface failure modes invisible to the primary persona — particularly around fluency level and trust posture.
Positive tests only

: zero-tolerance persona cells without negative test cases are the most dangerous gap in an eval suite. The model passing 100% of positive tests while silently hallucinating on edge cases is worse than a visible failure rate.
Static coverage map
: a coverage map authored at design time and never updated. Persona dimension scores drift as users gain experience with AI; the coverage map must be reviewed quarterly and updated to match.
LLM judge without calibration

: a generic LLM judge prompt that ignores persona dimension scores will apply a uniform quality bar. A judge that does not know the persona is Sceptic will not penalise missing citations — defeating the purpose of persona-driven design.
P0/P1 distinction ignored in CI

: treating all eval scenarios as equal in CI removes the signal. A P1 failure in CI should create a bug ticket; a P0 failure should block the merge. Both set to 'advisory' produces the same outcome as no evaluation at all.
Summary

Persona-driven eval scenario design starts with the persona x job coverage map — a matrix that makes visible which combinations are critical, which are secondary, and which are out of scope. Each non-empty cell requires four scenario types (happy path, boundary, negative, recovery), with the mix determined by the persona's failure tolerance. Dimension scores translate directly into pass/fail criteria: a Sceptic trust posture requires citation presence tests; zero failure tolerance requires negative hallucination tests. Claude generates structured scenarios using a tool-use schema, ensuring consistent JSON format suitable for a pytest-based CI pipeline. LLM-as-judge evaluation calibrates assessment to persona-specific criteria that regex cannot capture. P0 scenarios — drawn from zero-tolerance persona x critical-job cells — block merges; P1 scenarios are advisory.
